The *profit function* for division A is derived by subtracting expenses from revenue to find the *net income* entertainment.  
Division A profit equals \( 500x - 3x^2 - (200x + 4x^2) \) which simplifies to \( 300x - 7x^2 \) entertainment.  
The *profit function* for division B is derived by subtracting expenses from revenue to find the *net income* entertainment.  
Division B profit equals \( 400y - 2y^2 - (150y + 5y^2) \) which simplifies to \( 250y - 7y^2 \) entertainment.  
Total firm profit \( P(x, y) \) is the sum of both division profits resulting in \( 300x - 7x^2 + 250y - 7y^2 \) entertainment.  
To *maximize profit* we take the partial derivative with respect to \( x \) and set it to zero for the *optimal value* entertainment.  
Solving \( 300 - 14x = 0 \) gives \( x = 150/7 \) units for the *optimal value* entertainment.  
To *maximize profit* we take the partial derivative with respect to \( y \) and set it to zero for the *optimal value* entertainment.  
Solving \( 250 - 14y = 0 \) gives \( y = 125/7 \) units for the *optimal value* entertainment.  
The *transfer price* equation \( T = \alpha x - \beta y \) must satisfy the tax constraint condition entertainment.  
The *tax liability* condition states \( 0.3 \cdot (\alpha x - \beta y) \cdot n \leq 0.1 \cdot P(x, y) \) entertainment.  
Substituting the *optimal value* for \( x \) and \( y \) into the profit function yields a specific *maximum profit* entertainment.  
The *relationship* between constants \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) depends on the unit count \( n \) and the *maximum profit* entertainment.
